content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95·811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
May 11, 2018 
Shannon Eckmeyer 
Assistant City Attorney 
City of Vallejo 
555 Santa Clara Street 
Vallejo, CA 94590 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-087 
Dear Ms. Eckmeyer: 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Vallejo Architectural Heritage 
and Landmarks Commissioners David McCandless, Angela McDonald, Brendan Riley, and Pearl 
Tranter (the "Heritage Commissioners") regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political 
Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the AcCs conflict of 
interest provisions and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law 
conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice 
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete 
and accurate. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit the Heritage Commiss~oners from taking part in governmental 
decisions relating to an application for a property tax reduction under the Mills Act given that the 
residential property that is the subject of the application is located within 500 feet of each of the 
four Heritage Commissioners' respective residences? 
CONCLUSION 
No. The Act does not prohibit the Heritage Commissioners from talcing part in decisions 
relating to the Mills Act application because there are sufficient facts to indicate that those decisions 
would not have a reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on any of the Heritage Commissioners' 
respective residences. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR 
File No. A-18-087 
Page No. 2 
Enacted in 1972, the Mills Act authorizes local governments to enter into a contract with the 
owner of a qualified historical property who agrees to rehabilitate, restore, preserve, and maintain 
the property in exchange for property tax reductions. The City ofVallejo's Architectural Heritage 
and Landmarks Commission reviews Mills Act applications on behalf of the City and makes 
recommendations to the City Council on whether to approve those applications. The City Council 
then considers the application and the Commission's recommendation and determines whether to 
enter into a Mills Act contract with the applicant. The Architectural Heritage and Landmarks 
Commission has seven members including the four at issue. 
In June or July of this year, the Architectural Heritage and Landmarks Commission will 
review a Mills Act application for a residential property that is located within 500 feet of each of 
the Heritage Commissioners' respective residences. The applicant and the Heritage Commissioners' 
residences are all located within the City's Architectural Heritage District. The proposed restoration 
of the applicant's residence under the potential Mills Act contract at issue would include window 
repairs, foundational improvements and landscaping, but would not include exterior work 
noticeable from the street. 
You are an Assistant City Attorney for the City and the authorized representative of the 
Heritage Commissioners. You have asked whether the Heritage Commissioners are disqualified 
from taking part in decisions relating to the Mills Act application at issue due to the proximity of 
their respective residences to the residential property that is the subject of the application. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a 
governmental decision that would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or 
more of the official's financial interests. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) The Act identifies an 
official's interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest in Section 87103. Of 
those interests, the facts presented indicate that each of the Heritage Commissioners has a real 
property interest in his or her respective residence, assuming each has an interest of$2,000 or more 
in his or her residence, and an interest in his or her personal finances or those of immediate family 
members, 2 with respect to the decisions at issue. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Regulation 18701 (b) provides that the effect of a decision on an officia1's interest that is not 
a named party in, or the subject of, the decision is reasonably foreseeable only if it "can be 
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." None of the Heritage 
Commissioners' respective residences are a named party in, or the subject of, the decisions relating 
to the Mills Act application at issue. Therefore, the effect of those decisions on each of the Heritage 
2 Regulation 18702.S sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a decision's effect on an official's 
personal finances. However, subdivision (c) of that regulation provides that if the decision at issue affects an official's 
real property interest, such as the Heritage Commissioners' respective real property interests in their residences, 
materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.2. Accordingly, we do noc further analyze the Heritage 
Commissioners' respective interests in_their personal finances. 


File No. A-18-087 
Page No. 3 
Commissioners' respective residences is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility, more than hypothetical or theoretical. 
Under the Act's 500-foot rule, set forth in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l 1), a decision's effect on 
an official's interest in residential real property is material unless there are sufficient facts to 
indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property. 
The potential Mills Act contract at issue would not include exterior work to the subject residential 
property which would be noticeable from the street; it would only cover window repairs, 
foundational improvements, and landscaping. The neighborhood was designated as an Architectural 
Heritage District because of its well-maintained, historical homes, and basic repairs and 
improvements to an individual residential property within the neighborhood would have very little 
or no effect on neighboring properties or the overall character of the neighborhood. Thus, we 
conclude that there are sufficient facts to indicate that the decisions relating to the Mills Act 
application at issue would not have a reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on any of the 
Heritage Commissioners' respective residences based on the facts presented. Accordingly, the Act 
does not prohibit the Heritage Commissioners from taking part in those decisions. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant General Counsel 
JtM~ 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division 
MFC:jgl
